# Abstract {.unnumbered}

**Keywords:** financial intelligence, natural language processing, sentiment analysis,
large language models, event extraction, data engineering, Random Forest, backtesting,
Casablanca Stock Exchange.

---

The Moroccan stock market, represented by the Bourse des Valeurs de Casablanca (BVC),
encompasses more than seventy-six listed companies whose performance is closely tied
to national and international economic and financial news. In this context, the ability
to analyze financial press sentiment in real time and automatically identify events
likely to affect stock prices constitutes a decisive advantage for investors and analysts.

This final-year engineering project proposes the design and implementation of an intelligent
financial monitoring system based on a multi-layer data pipeline architecture. The system
continuously collects articles from thirteen RSS feeds of Moroccan and international economic
press, as well as extensive historical archives retrieved via the Internet Archive CDX API.
Each article is processed by a large language model (LLM) deployed locally via Ollama,
which extracts sentiment (positive, negative, neutral), confidence score, mentioned entities
(listed companies, sectors, people), event type from a taxonomy of thirteen categories,
and a deduplication fingerprint.

Collected data is stored in a silver layer using PostgreSQL and Neo4j, then transformed
nightly into an analytical gold layer exposing approximately eighty features per
(ticker, date) pair. These features combine price indicators (OHLCV), rolling-window
sentiment aggregates (3 and 7 days), binary event flags, and a one-hot representation
of LLM event types.

A supervised modeling pipeline trains a Random Forest classifier to predict next-day
price direction and a regressor to predict expected return. A backtesting framework
evaluates trading strategies generated by these signals, computing cumulative returns,
maximum drawdown, Sharpe ratio, and monthly performance heatmaps.

The entire system is orchestrated by Apache Airflow with four scheduled DAGs (hourly,
daily at 10:00, 17:00, and 02:00), and exposed via a Streamlit dashboard featuring
four pages: overview, execution tracking, live feed, and stock prices.

Results demonstrate the technical feasibility of a fully automated, cost-effective
(local model) and extensible financial monitoring system, providing a solid foundation
for future research in quantitative finance applied to the Moroccan market.
